coredata.dc:title: Synthesis, characterization of carbon dots from onion peel and their application as absorbent and anticancer activity
coredata.dc:creator: Salimi Shahraki, Hesam
Qurtulen,
Ahmad, Anees
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution through dyes has become a challenging issue all around the world. The recognition of the alarming industrial waste discharge and lack of access to fresh water has impressed us to develop a feasible dye adsorbent. In this regard, highly efficient and low-cost CDs were prepared using onion waste with quantum yield 12% as a prospective adsorbent to remove Congo red and Methyl orange. The optical and physicochemical properties of CDs have been characterized using UV, PL, FTIR, SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS and BET analyses. To optimize the maximum adsorption of Congo red and Methyl orange, an efficient and comparative study of the adsorption process was systematically tested using different parameters such as adsorbent dosage, pH, contact time, and initial dye concentration. From the obtained results, the maximum adsorption efficiency reached 91.53% and 91.09% for Congo red and Methyl orange, respectively. In addition, the equilibrium isotherm, kinetic study, and thermodynamics of the adsorption were calculated. According to the experimental equilibrium, the isotherm analysis is well-fitted for the Freundlich model. It is worth noting adsorption kinetics could be fitted with second-order kinetics models (R2 = 1) for CR and (R2 = 0.998) for MO. Finally, the anticancer activity of CDs against MCF-7 breast cancer cells is demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dye
Adsorption
Kinetic model
Anticancer
Thermodynamics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of temperature and thermodynamic studies
Effect of concentration and isotherm studies

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors acknowledge with thanks to the Department of Chemistry, AMU, Aligarh, for the financial support to carry out this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the synthesis method of carbon dots from onion peel.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs and (b) PL emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelength from 310 to 410 nm, (c) FTIR spectrum of CDs before and (d) after the adsorption of CR and MO.
(a) XRD analysis of CDs, (b) XPS spectrum of carbon dots, (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, (d) O1s of the CDs.

TEM image of CDs with different scales (a) 20 nm, (b) 5 nm, inset: particle size distribution graph of CDs, SEM image of CDs (c) before and (d) after the Congo red adsorption, (e) after the Methyl orange adsorption.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) pore width of the CDs measured by BET method.
Effects of pH (initial dye concentration: 20 ppm, amount of adsorbent = 0.02 g, volume of adsorbate = 50 mL).
Effect of dosage of CDs on the CR and MO adsorption. (initial dye concentration: 20 ppm, volume of adsorbate = 50 mL).
Effect of contact time on (a) CR and (b) MO adsorption onto CDs.
Effect of temperature on removal of CR and MO on CDs.
Plot of lnKd versus 1/T for the adsorption of (a) CR and (b) MO onto CDs.
Effect of initial dye concentration on (a) adsorption capacity (b) adsorption efficiency for CR and MO by CDs.
Pseudo-second-order kinetic plots for (a) the adsorption of CR and (b) MO by CDs.
Recycling efficiency of CDs for CR and MO removal.
Cell viability of CDs on MCF-7 (breast cancer) cells.
Confocal microscopy images of MCF-7 breast cancer cells after 24 h and 48 h under 510 nm excitation without or with CDs. Scale bar = 20 µm.
Thermodynamics parameters for the Congo Red (CR) and Methyl Orange (MO) adsorption by CDs. (volume of adsorbate = 50 mL, pH ∼ 4, amount of adsorbent = 0.02 g).
Comparison of adsorption capacities for removal of CR and MO by various adsorbents.
Adsorption isotherm parameters of CR and MO by CDs.
Error function analysis for the adsorption of CR and MO by CDs.

Adsorption kinetic parameters for CR and MO adsorption (a) PS1 kinetic parameters of CR, (b) MO, (c) PS2 kinetic parameters of CR, (d) MO (contact time = 30–120 min, initial dye concentration = 10–100 mg/L, Dosage of adsorbent = 0.02 g).

head.ce:title: Synthesis, characterization of carbon dots from onion peel and their application as absorbent and anticancer activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh 202002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution through dyes has become a challenging issue all around the world. The recognition of the alarming industrial waste discharge and lack of access to fresh water has impressed us to develop a feasible dye adsorbent. In this regard, highly efficient and low-cost CDs were prepared using onion waste with quantum yield 12% as a prospective adsorbent to remove Congo red and Methyl orange. The optical and physicochemical properties of CDs have been characterized using UV, PL, FTIR, SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS and BET analyses. To optimize the maximum adsorption of Congo red and Methyl orange, an efficient and comparative study of the adsorption process was systematically tested using different parameters such as adsorbent dosage, pH, contact time, and initial dye concentration. From the obtained results, the maximum adsorption efficiency reached 91.53% and 91.09% for Congo red and Methyl orange, respectively. In addition, the equilibrium isotherm, kinetic study, and thermodynamics of the adsorption were calculated. According to the experimental equilibrium, the isotherm analysis is well-fitted for the Freundlich model. It is worth noting adsorption kinetics could be fitted with second-order kinetics models (R2 = 1) for CR and (R2 = 0.998) for MO. Finally, the anticancer activity of CDs against MCF-7 breast cancer cells is demonstrated.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Dye is one of the most prevalent contaminants, which is omnipresent in many applications such as food industries, tannery, rubber, plastics, veterinary medicine, etc. [1]. More than 100,000 commercial dyes exist, and over 7 × 105 tons are produced annually [2,3]. Their large-scale production has caused the dye molecules in water to affect the penetration of solar light, which can severely impact the aquatic environment [4]. Water pollution, especially in a developing country, which increases because of untreated effluent discharged from industries into the aquatic environment, is of primary concern [5]. An efficient and selective method for the removal of dyes is becoming an increasingly serious challenge [6]. Furthermore, industries require a low-cost and plausible method for the treatment of dye-related water before entering main water streams. Many of these dyes are highly toxic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic effects [7,8]. Among all the dyes, Congo red (CR) and Methyl Orange (MO) have many adverse effects on aquatic flora, fauna, and human health. Azo dyes, including one or more azo bonds (
- N = N - )
, are the most commonly used dye, such as Congo red (CR) and Methyl Orange (MO). In addition, aromatic amines with azo groups can be highly carcinogenic to humans and animals [9–11]. Therefore, removing these life-threatening contaminants from wastewater is necessary before their final discharge into the environment.

Among all treatment techniques, adsorption has been identified as one of the most efficient methods for treating contaminated water and removal of organic pollutants in wastewater [12]. However, the adsorption process has been proven as low cost, simplicity in design, effectiveness in treating effluent, and easy operations. For this reason, suitable and cost-effective adsorbents should be assessed for the elimination of toxic pollutants like dye, not least because hazardous dyes in water may pose a threat to the environment and human health [13]. Currently, interest in using various conventional waste biomass has been increased by many researchers, such as Carbon Dots (CDs) prepared from various biomass waste, like fruit peels, sugarcane bagasse, rice hull, and wheat straw. Carbon present in CDs is an excellent adsorbent for the effective adsorption of dyes [14,15].

CDs are a novel and rising member of the carbon family with spectacular properties, such as high aqueous solubility and stability, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and wavelength-tunable fluorescence emission [16]. These exclusive properties made them widely applicable in photocatalysis, biosensors, bioimaging, etc. To date, several protocols like arc discharge, electrochemical method, microwave digestion, ultrasonic oscillation, and hydrothermal synthesis are used for the synthesis of CDs [17–20]. In an attempt to manage a soft condition and simple synthetic method for CDs preparation, Onion peel waste was adopted as a precursor to prepare CDs by one-step and green hydrothermal approaches with a quantum yield of 12 %.

Onion is one of the most consumed vegetables in the human diet with a long history of use in food and medicine. One third of the fresh weight of processed onions is discarded as waste. Additionally, 10% of harvested onions are worthlessly marketed, which means they do not meet the marketing standards [21,22]. Onion waste disposal presents several challenges for conventional ways of vegetable waste disposal, including: (1) onion waste cannot be used as fodder because of possible toxicity and particular aroma, (2) its composting is restricted due to the high amount of sulfur-containing compounds [23]. Herein, the use of CDs from onion peel as an adsorbent was investigated for the adsorption of CR and MO from an aqueous solution. In this regard, an efficient and comparative study of the adsorption process based on the effects of various operating parameters was examined. We also report the application of CDs as anticancer agents toward MCF-7 breast cancer cells.

In view of the above, we have presented green CDs that possess the following significant advantages; a) Eco-friendly adsorbent for removal of dyes from wastewater, b) Green synthesis through the hydrothermal method, c) The excellent ability to absorb anionic azo dye molecules with high efficiency [24,25]. This paper will provide proper directions for the environmental application of CDs and suggest guidelines for developing effective environmental pollution control and other environmental stewardship activities.

Ethylene diamine was purchased from Merck Chemicals India. Onion was collected from the local market. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Methyl orange (C14H14N3NaO3S, MW: 327.33 g/mol, λmax = 464.5 nm) and Congo red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2, MW: 696.66 g/mol, λmax = 497 nm) as adsorbate were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. CR and MO dye solutions were prepared in double distilled water (DDW) to a concentration of 1000 mg/L and All chemicals were used without further purifications.

The red onions were washed thoroughly and peeled off. 20 gm peels were then chopped and boiled in 100 mL of double distilled water (DDW) for 30 min and stirred with the help of a magnetic stirrer for 2 h. The aqueous extract of peels was cooled down at room temperature and filtered through a sintered glass filter. 0.6 mL ethylene diamine (EDA) was dissolved in 20 mL and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 120 ◦C for 150 min. Then obtained, a brown-colored liquid was filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove the larger particles. The obtained CDs were kept and maintained at 4 °C till further use. Sometimes it was necessary to prepare CDs as a solid for further characterization and applications. In this case, an amount of CDs solution was isolated and vacuum freeze-dried for 48 h to produce the solid brown powder. The scheme for the synthesis of CDs from onion peel is presented in Fig. 1 .

The optical properties of the sample were examined by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer LAMBDA-45) and PL spectra (HITACHI-F2500). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer IR spectrometer) was used to define the main functional groups of adsorbent before and after adsorption. The crystallographic structure of the adsorbent was identified by X-Ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku TTRAX III). The surface morphology was obtained by using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOL-JSM 6510). The morphology of adsorbent was figured out using transmission electron microscopy (JEOL-JEM 2100). The XPS spectra measurements were achieved on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI 5000 Versa Probe III). The BET method (BELSORP MINI II) was applied to define the specific surface areas, pore volume, and pore size. Confocal microscopy (Zeiss, Germany) was used to determine the complete structure of objects within the cell.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated by comparing the integrated emission intensity with quinine sulfate as the standard (QY of 54%) in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 360 nm excitation wavelength based on Eq. (1):
(1)
Q
CD
=
Q R
·
I
CD
I R
·
A R
A
CD
·
η
CD
2
η R 2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index, A is the optical density. The subscripts “R” and “CD” refer to the reference substance (Quinine sulfate) and the CDs sample. The value of η is 1.33 for those two solutions. The absorbance of CDs and quinine solutions were kept below 0.10 to ensure a linear relationship between the molar concentration and the absorbance.

The adsorption studies were carried out under different conditions for optimization, contact time, temperature, adsorbent dose, the effect of pH and initial dye concentration on the adsorption of Congo red (CR) and methyl orange (MO) on CDs. A sufficient amount of CR and MO (1 g) dye were dissolved in DDW to form a series of anticipated concentrations. Further desired concentration is prepared by dilution from the stock solution. The appropriate amount of CDs was added to the dye solution under stirring with a speed of 300 rpm at room temperature to obtain equilibrium. The concentration of CR and MO were investigated at wavelength of maximum absorption (λmax) 497 nm for CR and 464 for MO [26,27]. The percentage dye adsorbed (R%) and equilibrium capacity (qe) of the dye were calculated by Eqs. (2) and (3):
(2)
R % =
(
C i
-
C e
)
C i
∗
100
(3)
q
e
m g
g
=
( C
i
-
C e
)
.
V
M
where Ci and Ce (mg/L) are the initial and equilibrium concentration of CR and MO (mg/L). V is volume of dye solution used (L), M is the mass of the adsorbent (g).

The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission spectra (PL) of the CDs were recorded in order to investigate their optical properties. The UV–vis absorbance range was wide with a broad peak at 280 nm and 320 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of (CC) bands (sp2 hybridization) and n → π* transition band of the (CO) respectively (Fig. 2 a). This is a common CDs absorption characteristic [28]. Fig. 2b exhibited the PL intensity of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths from 310 to 410 nm. The maximum emission peak is around 380 nm, which is observed for an excitation wavelength of 410 nm. Based on these results, the PL intensity of CDs is related to the excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength was changed from 310 to 410 nm in 20 nm increments, the emission peaks shifted to a longer wavelength because of the photoinduced electrons and holes present in CDs at various surfaces of energy traps [29].

FT-IR spectroscopic technique was employed to identify the characteristic functional groups of the CDs. As presented in Fig. 2c, a broad peak at 3468 cm−1 is attributed to O–H stretching. The presence of a prominent peak at 1610 cm−1 referred to the aromatic CC stretching in the CDs structure. The peaks located around 980 cm−1 and 610 cm−1 show the presence of strong CC and CH bending. The FTIR spectra of CDs after adsorption of CR and MO can be observable in Fig. 2d. Significant changes in functional group frequencies were seen after the adsorption of CR and MO dyes at the surface of CDs because of participation in the sorption process, either physical adsorption (van der Waals adsorption) or chemisorption [30]. The broad bands at 3423 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 in both cases are assigned to stretching mode of –OH and N–H bond, respectively. The peaks at 2921 cm−1 indicate the functional groups of aliphatic CH stretching. The peak at 1031 cm−1 can be assigned to C-O of carboxylates.

The XRD pattern of the CDs demonstrated a single broad peak centered at 2
θ =
21.1°, which corresponds to the (002) peak (Fig. 3 a). The result showed that the CDs interlayer spacing (d) (0.42 nm) is greater than the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm) along with the broadness character, which is confirmed the generation of more oxygen-containing groups due to the poor crystalline nature of CDs [30]. Fig. 3b displayed the XPS survey spectrum of CDs, which shows two intense peaks located at 282.5 eV and 530.02 eV, related to C1s and O1s of the synthesized CDs, respectively. The C1s spectra (Fig. 3c) can be subdivided into two peaks at 283.4 eV and 284.05 eV, which are attributed to C-O and C–C, respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. 3d) depicted two peaks at about 529.53 eV and 530.92 eV, corresponding to C-O and CO/O-CO, respectively.

The TEM image of CDs indicated that the particles were well dispersed and uniform, displaying near spherical morphology (Fig. 4 a). The high-resolution TEM of CDs (Fig. 4b) showed the diameter size of CDs ranges from 4 to 6 nm and the average diameter of CDs is around 5 nm. Fig. 4c demonstrated the SEM morphology of the pure CDs. As shown in the figure, the CDs are nearly spherical, with uniform distribution and size, and it seems to have a strong agglomeration effect. Fig. 4d and (e) are the SEM images of CDs after CR and MO adsorption. It could observe from the figures that a thick layer of dye covered the surface and pores, as a result, the roughness of the adsorbent was decreased. This is consistent with other researchers who found surface morphological changes after dye adsorption [31].

The specific surface area, pore volume, and average pore size of the adsorbent were investigated by the BET method at 77 K of liquid nitrogen. N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and the width of the pore size distribution of adsorbent were also characterized by BET method and showed in Fig. 5 a and (b). The surface area, pore-volume, and average pore size of the adsorbent were 4.70 m2 g−1, 0.0031 cm3 g−1, and 6.95 nm, respectively.

pH is an important controlling parameter that affects the dye adsorption process. The effect of pH on the Congo red and Methyl orange adsorption on CDs was examined by adapting the different pH of dye solution in the range of 2–10. The experiment involved preparing 0.02 g of adsorbent in 50 mL of dye solution with the desired initial concentration. At low pH values, the color is changed to violet and red for CR and MO, respectively because of the protonation of β nitrogen of the azo dye [32]. The adsorption efficiency of both dyes declines with increasing the pH value. The high adsorption efficiency of CR and MO were obtained 80.6 % and 76.7 % at pH 4, respectively (Fig. 6 ). It might be at higher pH, the number of negatively charged sites existed on the surface of the adsorbent. A negatively charged surface does not favor the adsorption of anionic dyes because of the electrostatic repulsion [33]. On the other hand, the excess of
O H
-
ions competes effectively with anionic dye for the limited adsorption sites in the basic aqueous solution. As a result, pH = 4 was chosen as the optimized pH for future studies.

The effect of adsorbent dosage for adsorption of both dyes was carried out by taking 50 mL dye solution and different the adsorbent dosages (0.01, 0.02, and 0.03 g). The effect of adsorbent dosage on the adsorption efficiency of CR and MO was demonstrated. The adsorption efficiency percentage increases up to a certain limit and then remains almost constant, as depicted in Fig. 7 . The initial increase in the efficiency of adsorption may be attributed to an enhancement in the number of available adsorption sites with an increase in adsorbent dose. After 0.02 g adsorbent dosage, the adsorption efficiency of the dye becomes almost constant, showing that equilibrium is achieved, and no further removal takes place as the adsorbent increases. In the subsequent studies, the adsorbent dose chosen was 0.02 g.

The effect of optimum contact time on the adsorption of CR and MO was conducted using 10–100 mg L−1 dye solution at constant temperature (temperature range from 30 °C to 40 °C) and different time intervals (30–120 min). It was observed that the adsorption capacity of CR and MO increased with increasing contact time. Initially, the adsorption of anionic dyes was rapid within the first 30 min, Onward 30 min, dye adsorption was continued with no further increase until 120 min (Fig. 8 a and (b)). The impact of agitation time shows a strong interaction happens between the dye molecules and the active adsorption sites on the adsorbent surface. After 30 min, the adsorption capacity is gradually constant until the adsorption reaches equilibrium. This is due to the adsorbed molecules of dye saturating all active sites on the adsorbent surface. Based on these results, it can be inferred that time has an important influence on dye adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and thermodynamic studies
ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is one of the most significant parameters to be considered in the adsorption of dyes. The effect of temperature on the removal of CR and MO by CDs was performed at different temperatures (303 to 333). Fig. 9 displays the relationship between adsorption efficiency and temperature. When the temperature increased from 303 to 333, the adsorption efficiency of CR increased from 66.3% to 72.1%. The reason could be due to the increased external and intra-particle diffusion rate of the adsorbate molecules into the surface [34]. It is found that the higher temperature favors conditions for the adsorption of CR. In the case of MO, increasing temperature was seen to lead to an increase in the adsorption efficiency and decline after 325 K. It has been well documented that surface destruction above 325 K and the small MO size easily move toward the surface compared to the CR at a high temperature.

The thermodynamic parameters (ΔG°, ΔH°, and ΔS°) on the adsorption of CR and MO dye were calculated from temperature dependent adsorption by using Eqs. (4), (5) and (6):
(4)
K d
=
q e
C e
(5)
Δ G ° = -
RTlnK d
(6)
ln
K d
=
Δ S °
R
-
Δ H °
RT
where R is the standard atmospheric pressure (8.314 J.mol−1.K−1), T is the absolute temperature of the solution, Kd is distribution coefficient, ΔGo is standard free energy, ΔSo, and ΔHo are Change in entropy and enthalpy respectively. The values of ΔS° and ΔH° were obtained from the slope and intercept of the plot of lnKd against 1/T, respectively (Fig. 10 a and Fig. 10b). During this study, the ΔH values of CR and MO are −4.4526 (kJmol−1) and 4.084 (kJmol−1), respectively. The positive ΔH proved that the adsorption process is endothermic, and the negative ΔH confirmed the exothermic nature of the process, whereas the positive values of ΔSo confirmed the randomness of the adsorption process. The thermodynamic calculations ΔG° values being negative for CR and MO, indicate that the adsorption process of both dyes at the surface of the CDs were established as feasible and spontaneous. The negative ΔG° values for CR were increased from −6.016 (kJ mol−1) to −7.0307 (kJ mol−1) and decreased from −3.582 (kJ mol−1) to −3.484 (kJ mol−1) for MO, with increasing the temperature from 303 to 333 K. These investigations confirmed that when the negative value decreases, the process becomes more feasible. The obtained thermodynamic parameter is given in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration and isotherm studies
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of initial dye concentrations of CR and MO on CDs by using an initial adsorbent dose of 0.02 g CDs over the range 10–100 (mg/L) at optimum pH and constant temperature were investigated as illustrated in Fig. 11 . The adsorption capacity was impressively dependent on the initial dye concentrations of CR and MO in the solution. It can be seen that the dye concentration increased, and the adsorption capacity initially increased and reached maximum at a point due to the mass transfer driving force would become larger. With the increase of initial dye concentration, the adsorption efficiency of CR and MO has shown a downward trend. The result owing to the decrease in adsorption efficiency with increasing initial dye concentration might be due to the adsorption sites were rapidly occupied. At lower concentration ranges, the percentage of dye removal is higher, whereas for high concentration, the fractional removal is always higher [33]. A comparison of the maximum adsorption capacities of CR and MO by various adsorbents is depicted in Table 2 .

The adsorption isotherm provides information about the adsorption mechanism and adsorption performance of the adsorbent. Types of adsorption isotherms (Langmuir, Freundlich, and Temkin models) were employed to examine the equilibrium data. The constants of isotherm models of CR and MO are shown in Table 3 . The Langmuir isotherm model is appropriate for single-layer adsorption on a homogeneous surface, and no interaction occurs between the adsorbents. The equation to describe the Langmuir isotherm is expressed as follows (7):
(7)
C e
q e
=
1
q
m
K L
+
C e
q m
where Ce and qe are related to the equilibrium concentration of dye in solution after adsorption (mg/L) and the adsorption capacity of the sample (mg/g), respectively. qm is described as maximum adsorption capacity and KL is the Langmuir constant. The Freundlich isotherm model was used to assess adsorption behavior and declared the adsorption accrues in multilayer surfaces of adsorbent with an energy diversity. The Freundlich isotherm is reported in the following Eqs. (8) and (9):
(8)
q e
=
K F
.
C
e
1 / n
(9)
l o g
q
e
= l o g
K F
+
1 n
log
C e

n and Kf are adsorption intensity and Freundlich constant, respectively. According to the heterogeneity factor n, the adsorption is linear (n = 1), when (n < 1) is a chemical process or a physical process (n > 1) [35]. The value of the heterogeneity factor is bigger than 1 in this paper representing that the adsorption of both dyes at the surface of CDs is physical in nature. The R2 value (1 for CR and 0.9989 for MO) of the Freundlich isotherm model indicates that the Freundlich model was best fitted than Langmuir and Temkin models. Based on the results heterogeneous nature of the adsorbent surface resulting multilayer adsorption of CR and MO molecules. Temkin isotherm assumes a linear decline in the adsorption heat rather than logarithm while neglecting extremely low and high concentrations. The following equation gives the temkin isotherm model (10):
(10)
q e
= B l n A + B l n
C e
where B = (RT/bT), constant related to the sorption heat (J/mol), bT is the temkin constant, A and T are the equilibrium binding constant (L/g) and the temperature (K) respectively.

Error function analysis for the adsorption of CR and MO is done to verify and check the correctness of adsorption data fitness to used isotherms and the respective predictions of models. This study calculated four types of error functions, including the χ2 (Chi-squared test), SAE (Sum of Absolute Error), MPSD (Marquardt's Percent Standard Deviation), and HYBRID (Hybrid Fractional Error Function), as seen in Table 4 . The values of error functions were found to be smaller for Freundlich than Langmuir and Temkin isotherm, while the R2 value was found to be closer to unity for the Freundlich isotherm compared to the Langmuir and Temkin isotherm model. It can be determined from the above discussion that CR and MO dye on CDs is better described by the Freundlich isotherm model.

Adsorption kinetics has focused on the adsorption and desorption rates, and mathematical models described this behavior. The kinetic studies of CR and MO onto CDs for the concentration of 10–100 (mg/L) were examined by the Pseudo-first order (PS1) and Pseudo-second order (PS2) model. The PS1 and PS2 rate models are defined as the following Eqs. (11) and (12):
(11)
log
q e
-
q t
= log
q e
-
k 1
2.303
t
(12)
t
q t
=
1
2
k 2
q
e
2
+
1
q e
where k1 and k2 (min−1) are the rate constant of PS1 and PS2, qe (mg/g) and qt (mg/g) are the quantity of CR and MO at a time (min) and equilibrium. The intercept and slope of the plot of log (qe − qt) versus time were determined by the values of k1 and qe, respectively. Where k2 (min−1) is the rate. The slope of the plot of t/qt versus t gives the values k2 and qe, respectively. (Fig. 12 a and (b)). The coefficient of determination (R2) of PS2 for CR and MO was ≤ 0.998 for every dye concentration. Thus, the adsorption of both dyes onto CDs was better simulated by the PS2 model than PS1 models. At PS2, dye concentration increased with increasing the value of qe might be due to the high competition for vacant sites, leading to higher sorption rates. The chemical reaction appears significant in the rate-controlling step in this type of adsorption, and the PS2 chemical reaction kinetics provides the best correlation of the experimental data. [42]. The parameters of the PS1 and PS2 models were calculated and listed in Table 5 .

Reusability of the CDs in the CR and MO removal was studied for four successive cycles. After every cycle, the adsorbent was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with ethanol for desorption, and dried at 70 °C for 6 h. Fig. 13 showed the maximum removal efficiency was 85 % for CR and 82 % for MO. The percentage of removal efficiency decreased up to 58 % for CR and 49 % for MO in fourth cycle as the number of cycles increased. The reduction in % removal efficiency could be due to the saturation of active sites on adsorbent surfaces. The results indicated that the CDs could be reused up to fourth cycle successfully.

The cytotoxic effect of synthesized CDs on the viability of breast cancer cells was determined by MTT assay. Briefly, exponentially growing breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cell line) were dispensed into 96-well plates at density of 1.0 × 105 cells per well and cultured for 24 h at 37˚C in a humidified chamber with 5% CO2. The cells were then incubated with varying concentrations of synthesized CDs. The untreated cells were taken as control. After 48 h of incubation, 5 mg/mL of (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was added to each well. The precipitated formazan was dissolved in 100 μl Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) after 4 h incubation, and the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) measured the absorbance at 570 nm. Cell viability ratio was calculated using the following Eq. (13):
(13)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
r a t i o
( % )
=
O D
t r e a t e d
O D
c o n t r o l
× 100

The MTT assay is a colorimetric assay for measuring cell viability and indicates the cytotoxic effect of potential compounds. MTT, a yellow dye, gets converted into formazan by the mitochondrial dehydrogenase enzyme. Fig. 14 shows the effect of increasing concentrations of CDs on breast cancer cells’ viability. The treatment with CDs for 48 h markedly inhibited breast cancer cells. The survival of the cancer cells was inversely correlated with concentration of the CDs, indicating a dose-dependent inhibitory effect. The IC50 of CDs was ∼ 64 μg/ml. To validate the efficacy of anticancer activity against MCF-7, we used as a probe for confocal scanning microscope with MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. 15 , after incubation for 48 h with MCF-7 cells, the cells with CDs became bright under the excitation of 510 nm laser. In contrast, the MCF-7 cells without CDs (control) almost displayed no fluorescence signal.

This investigation proposes the successful and green synthesis of CDs from onion peel through a simple hydrothermal method. The green synthesized CDs with an average size of 5.25 nm and a reasonable quantum yield (12%) compared to other green synthesized CDs that have been examined. In this study, the functionality of synthesized CDs has been investigated in two directions particularly in the adsorption of Congo red and Methyl orange dye. The surface morphology, optical property, crystalline structure, and adsorption behavior of CDs are all characterized. The adsorption efficiency of both dyes was found to decrease with an increase in the initial dye concentration solution and adsorbent dosage. The percent adsorption efficiency of CR increased with an increase in temperature, but in the case of MO, adsorption efficiency decreased when the temperature was increased. The maximum adsorption efficiency of CR and MO was obtained at 91.53% and 91.09%, respectively. Equilibrium data were fitted to the Freundlich adsorption isotherm model suggesting multilayer adsorption of CR and MO molecules occurred. The pseudo-second-order model fitted well, the kinetics data implying that chemisorption is the rate-determining step. Moreover, CDs were not only used for the removal of dyes from aqueous solution but also could act as anticancer agents against MCF-7 (human breast cancer) cells and open a new way to apply synergistically.

Hesam Salimi Shahraki: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation, Investigation. Qurtulen: Data curation, Reviewing and editing. Anees Ahmad: Supervision, Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge with thanks to the Department of Chemistry, AMU, Aligarh, for the financial support to carry out this work.
